posts.title: AI agents break in production GTM
posts.hook: Everyone's shipping AI agents for dev workflows. Almost nobody's shipping them for GTM.
posts.body:
Everyone's shipping AI agents for dev workflows. Almost nobody's shipping them for GTM.

And the ones that exist? They demo beautifully. Then they hallucinate a prospect's company name in an outbound email and you lose the account.

I've been building AI agents that run my entire GTM pipeline. Enrichment, scoring, routing, content distribution. Here's what I've learned after 3 months in production:

1. The agent isn't the hard part. The data pipeline feeding it is.
2. You need circuit breakers everywhere. One bad API response cascades into 200 wrong emails if you're not careful.
3. Deterministic steps should stay deterministic. Don't let an LLM decide which CRM field to update. Let it decide what to write in that field.
4. Logging everything is non-negotiable. When something breaks at 2am on a cron job, you need the receipts.

The Karpathy point about reliability being the real challenge? That hits different when your agent is emailing real prospects, not generating code you can review before shipping.

GTM agents have a smaller margin for error than dev agents. A bad code suggestion gets caught in review. A bad email gets caught by your prospect's CEO.
posts.cta: What's the worst AI agent failure you've seen in a production workflow?

inspired_by.author: Andrej Karpathy
inspired_by.summary: Karpathy shares insights on building production-grade AI agents, focusing on reliability challenges in automation tasks. He outlines key developer tools needed for training and fine-tuning agentic models. Touches on infrastructure requirements for low-latency inference.

remixes.label: failure-autopsy
remixes.hook: My AI agent sent a cold email to the wrong person at the right company. Here's the postmortem.
remixes.body:
My AI agent sent a cold email to the wrong person at the right company. Here's the postmortem.

The enrichment step pulled the CTO's name from an outdated data source. The personalization layer referenced a blog post that CTO wrote in 2023. The email went to the new CTO, who had no idea what we were talking about.

The prospect replied: "I think you meant my predecessor."

That single failure taught me more about production AI than six months of building. The problem wasn't the LLM. It was the data pipeline feeding it stale context and my blind trust that enrichment APIs return current information.

Three things I changed after that:

1. Every enrichment result gets a freshness score. If the source data is older than 90 days, it gets flagged for human review before it hits an outbound sequence.

2. I stopped letting the agent compose full emails autonomously. It drafts. I approve. The 30 seconds of review saves hours of damage control.

3. I built a validation layer that cross-references LinkedIn profile data against company page data. If there's a mismatch on title or tenure, the record gets quarantined.

AI agents in GTM aren't a set-and-forget play. They're a "trust but verify" play. The agent does 90% of the work. Your job is catching the 10% that would embarrass you.
remixes.cta: What's your worst AI-generated outbound mistake? The ones we talk about are the ones we learn from.

remixes.label: build-vs-buy-angle
remixes.hook: I evaluated 11 AI GTM tools before building my own. None of them understood my pipeline.
remixes.body:
I evaluated 11 AI GTM tools before building my own. None of them understood my pipeline.

Every vendor demo showed the same thing: feed it a list, it writes emails, magic happens. But when I asked basic questions like "how does it handle conflicting data from two enrichment sources?" or "what happens when the CRM record was updated by a human after enrichment?", I got blank stares.

The gap isn't intelligence. These tools are smart enough. The gap is context. A generic AI agent doesn't know that when I say "qualified," I mean something specific to my ICP. It doesn't know that my sales cycle starts on Reddit, not in a BDR's inbox.

So I built agents that fit my actual workflow. Not a platform. Just scripts.

A scoring agent that weighs signals I care about. An enrichment agent that knows which data sources I trust and which I don't. A routing agent that sends warm leads to my CRM and cold ones to a nurture queue.

Total cost: my time plus about $40/month in API calls.

The insight that changed everything: AI agents for GTM only work when they're opinionated about your specific business. Generic agents produce generic results. And generic outbound gets ignored.
remixes.cta: Have you tried building custom AI agents for your GTM workflow, or are you still evaluating vendor tools? What's blocking you either way?

tones.advisor:
Everyone's shipping AI agents for dev workflows. Almost nobody's shipping them for GTM.

And based on what I've seen consulting with GTM teams this year, there's a reason. The failure modes are different. A dev agent that writes bad code gets caught by tests. A GTM agent that writes bad outbound gets caught by your prospect. After they've already formed an opinion about you.

I've been running AI agents across my entire GTM pipeline for three months now. Enrichment, scoring, routing, content distribution. Here's what I'd recommend if you're considering this path:

First, start with internal workflows, not customer-facing ones. Let the agent score leads and route records before you let it draft emails. Build trust in the system before you put it in front of buyers.

Second, build validation layers from day one. Every agent output that touches a prospect should have a confidence score and a human review step. Remove the human review only after you've verified accuracy over hundreds of runs.

Third, treat your GTM agents like junior employees. They're fast and tireless, but they lack judgment. The architecture should reflect that. Let them draft, research, and prioritize. Keep final approval with someone who understands the relationship.

Everyone's shipping AI agents for dev workflows. Almost nobody's shipping them for GTM.

Because GTM leaders are terrified. And honestly, they should be.

Dev agents fail quietly. A bad code suggestion gets caught in review. Nobody outside your team sees it. GTM agents fail loudly. A hallucinated company name lands in a prospect's inbox and suddenly your brand is the cautionary tale on LinkedIn.

But here's what nobody wants to admit: your human BDRs are already making these mistakes. They're copying the wrong name from Salesforce. They're referencing funding rounds that happened two years ago. They're sending "personalized" emails that sound like every other "personalized" email.

The difference? When a human screws up, you call it a coaching opportunity. When an AI screws up, you call it a catastrophic failure and shelve the project.

I've been running AI agents across my entire GTM pipeline for three months. Enrichment, scoring, routing, content distribution. The error rate is lower than what I had before. Not zero. Lower.

The teams avoiding AI agents in GTM aren't being cautious. They're falling behind while telling themselves it's a strategy.

platforms.newsletter:
Something I've been thinking about a lot lately.

Everyone's building AI agents for developer workflows. Code review, testing, deployment. Makes sense. The feedback loops are tight and the failures are private.

But almost nobody's building them for GTM. And the few that exist? They look incredible in demos. Then they hallucinate a prospect's company name in a live outbound email.

I've been running AI agents across my full pipeline for three months now. Enrichment, scoring, routing, content distribution. I've learned more about where AI breaks than most vendor pitch decks will ever tell you.

I'm going to break down what actually works, what fails dangerously, and how I've structured the whole thing to keep the failure blast radius small.

posts.title: Ship in a week or don't ship
posts.hook: The best GTM tool I built this year took 4 days. The worst one took 6 weeks.
posts.body:
The best GTM tool I built this year took 4 days. The worst one took 6 weeks.

The 4-day tool: a Reddit scout that finds conversations where people are asking for exactly what I sell. Pulls posts, scores relevance with a local LLM, queues drafts for me to approve. Runs on a cron. Cost me $0/month after the initial build.

The 6-week tool: a "comprehensive" lead scoring engine with 14 weighted signals, custom UI, three different data sources, and a beautiful dashboard nobody looks at.

The pattern is obvious in hindsight. The fast build solved one problem well. The slow build tried to solve every problem eventually.

Pieter Levels ships products in a week because he picks a single use case and builds exactly that. No configurability for hypothetical users. No admin panel for edge cases that might happen.

I started applying this to internal tools and it changed everything. One script. One job. One output. If it works, add a second thing. If it doesn't, delete it and move on.

Most GTM teams are running 6-week projects that should be 4-day scripts.
posts.cta: What's the simplest tool in your stack that drives the most value?

inspired_by.author: Arvid Kahl
inspired_by.summary: Arvid recounted his journey pivoting from corporate job to bootstrapping his first SaaS product as a solo founder. He outlined key lessons on validating ideas quickly and shipping MVPs without a team. Shared metrics from his first 100 customers.

remixes.label: kill-your-roadmap
remixes.hook: I deleted my product roadmap and replaced it with a one-week rule. If I can't ship it in a week, I don't start it.
remixes.body:
I deleted my product roadmap and replaced it with a one-week rule. If I can't ship it in a week, I don't start it.

Sounds reckless. It's the opposite.

Before the rule, I spent six weeks building a lead scoring system with 14 weighted signals, custom decay curves, and a beautiful dashboard. By the time it was done, my ICP had shifted and half the signals were irrelevant.

After the rule, I built a Reddit scout in four days. It pulls posts from six subreddits, scores them with a local LLM, and queues draft responses for my review. Runs on a cron. Costs nothing after the initial build.

The Reddit scout has generated more pipeline than the lead scoring engine ever did.

The pattern I keep seeing: the tools that take weeks to build are optimizing for completeness. The tools that take days to build are optimizing for one specific job. And in early-stage GTM, specificity beats completeness every time.

My rule now: define the single job the tool needs to do. Build the minimum version that does that job. Ship it. Run it for two weeks. If it works, iterate. If it doesn't, you lost four days instead of six weeks.

The constraint isn't time. It's clarity about what actually matters.
remixes.cta: What's the last tool you built in under a week that outperformed something you spent months on?

remixes.label: complexity-tax
remixes.hook: Every feature you add to an internal tool is a tax you pay every time it breaks.
remixes.body:
Every feature you add to an internal tool is a tax you pay every time it breaks.

I learned this the expensive way. Built a lead scoring engine over six weeks. Fourteen weighted signals. Custom decay functions. Configurable thresholds. It was genuinely impressive engineering.

Then the enrichment API changed their response format and the whole thing collapsed. Took me two days to debug because the complexity made the failure point invisible.

Meanwhile, my Reddit scout, built in four days, has been running without intervention for months. It does one thing. It pulls posts, scores relevance, queues drafts. When something breaks, I can find the problem in five minutes because there's almost no code.

The complexity tax isn't just build time. It's maintenance time, debugging time, and the cognitive overhead of remembering how all the pieces connect.

I've started grading my internal tools on what I call the "vacation test." If the tool breaks while I'm on vacation, can I fix it from my phone in 10 minutes? If not, it's too complex.

The Reddit scout passes the vacation test. The lead scoring engine never would have.

Simple tools that run reliably beat sophisticated tools that require babysitting. Every single time.
remixes.cta: What's the most over-engineered internal tool you've built or seen? What would the simple version look like?

The best GTM tool I built this year took 4 days. The worst one took 6 weeks.

Here's the framework I'd recommend for deciding what to build and how long to spend on it.

The 4-day tool was a Reddit scout. One job: find conversations where people are asking for what I sell. It pulls posts, scores relevance with a local LLM, queues drafts for approval. Runs on a cron. Zero ongoing cost.

The 6-week tool was a lead scoring engine with 14 weighted signals. By the time I shipped it, the market had moved and the assumptions baked into those weights were stale.

My recommendation: apply a strict one-week time box to any internal tool build. If you can't define the core value prop and ship a working version in a week, you're solving the wrong problem or solving it at the wrong altitude.

The tools that create disproportionate value tend to share three traits. They do exactly one job. They run without supervision. And they cost almost nothing to maintain.

The 6-week tool was a lead scoring engine I built because I convinced myself I needed "comprehensive" scoring with 14 weighted signals. You know what 14 weighted signals gets you? A system so complex that when it breaks, you can't tell if it's the enrichment layer, the scoring logic, or the weight configuration that's wrong.

Here's what nobody in the "build sophisticated systems" crowd wants to hear: your users don't care about your architecture. Your pipeline doesn't care about your elegant abstractions. Results come from tools that actually run, not tools that impress other engineers.

The startup graveyard is full of beautifully engineered internal tools that nobody maintained past month two because the person who built them moved on and nobody else could understand the codebase.

Ship in a week or question whether you should ship at all. Complexity is not a feature. It's a liability.

posts.title: Personalization theater is dead
posts.hook: Outbound personalization in 2026 is mostly theater. The prospect knows it. You know it.
posts.body:
Outbound personalization in 2026 is mostly theater. The prospect knows it. You know it.

"I saw your company just raised a Series B, congrats!" is not personalization. It's a trigger event you pulled from Crunchbase that 40 other vendors also pulled from Crunchbase.

Real personalization requires understanding what someone actually cares about right now. Not what their company announced. What they're struggling with.

Here's how I actually personalize at scale:

I monitor Reddit, LinkedIn, and niche communities for people describing problems my services solve. Not company news. Their words. Their frustrations. Their specific technical questions.

Then I engage in those conversations with genuine answers. No pitch. No CTA. Just useful context from someone who's built the thing they're asking about.

The conversion rate on "I helped you solve a problem last week" is infinitely higher than "I noticed your company expanded into EMEA."

Josh Braun is right that outbound automation fails without intent data. But most people define intent as "visited our pricing page." Real intent is someone posting "how do I connect Clay to my CRM without breaking our lead routing" at 11pm on a Tuesday.
posts.cta: When's the last time a cold email actually felt personal to you? What made it different?

inspired_by.author: Josh Braun
inspired_by.summary: Explains why outbound automation fails without personalized intent data. Outlines a 3-step process for humanizing automated outreach to boost reply rates by 40%. Warns against over-reliance on tools like Apollo without strategy.

remixes.label: reverse-the-funnel
remixes.hook: The best outbound I've sent this year didn't mention the prospect's company once.
remixes.body:
The best outbound I've sent this year didn't mention the prospect's company once.

No "congrats on the Series B." No "I noticed you're hiring for X role." No trigger event pulled from a database that 40 other vendors also have access to.

Instead, I replied to something the prospect actually wrote. A comment they left on a Reddit thread about a problem I solve. Their own words, their own frustration, their own context.

That's the difference between personalization and recognition. Personalization says "I know facts about your company." Recognition says "I understand what you're dealing with right now."

Most outbound tools are built for personalization. They pull firmographic data, reference recent news, and insert merge fields. The output looks personal. But the prospect knows it's automated because 12 other vendors sent the same "congrats on the funding" email that week.

Recognition requires something harder: actually finding the places where your prospects are talking about their problems and engaging with what they said. Not at scale. Not automated. Just present.

I've shifted my entire outbound strategy away from trigger-based sequences and toward what I call "signal-first" outreach. Find the signal that someone cares about this problem right now. Then respond to that signal specifically.

The response rates aren't even comparable.
remixes.cta: What's the most genuinely personal cold outreach you've ever received? What made it stand out?

remixes.label: personalization-stack-teardown
remixes.hook: I audited my outbound personalization stack. 80% of what I thought was "personalized" was the same data every competitor had.
remixes.body:
I audited my outbound personalization stack. 80% of what I thought was "personalized" was the same data every competitor had.

Here's what I found:

Layer 1: Firmographic data. Company size, industry, location, funding stage. Every sales tool on the market has this. Zero differentiation.

Layer 2: Trigger events. New hires, funding rounds, product launches. Better, but Crunchbase, LinkedIn, and a dozen intent data providers sell this to everyone. Your "timely" outreach arrives alongside 30 other "timely" messages.

Layer 3: Content signals. What the prospect actually writes, comments on, shares, and engages with. Their own words about their own problems. Almost nobody uses this because it doesn't scale neatly into a sequence tool.

The problem with modern outbound isn't that it's not personalized enough. It's that everyone is personalizing from the same data sources. When your input data is identical to your competitor's input data, your output will be too. No matter how good your AI writer is.

I rebuilt my outreach around Layer 3 exclusively. I use scouts to find where my ICP is actively talking about problems I solve. Reddit threads, LinkedIn comments, community posts. Then I engage with what they actually said.

It doesn't scale the way sequence tools do. But the conversion rate makes the math work anyway.
remixes.cta: Which layer of personalization does your outbound actually rely on? Be honest.

Outbound personalization in 2026 is mostly theater. The prospect knows it. You know it.

Here's my recommendation for teams serious about standing out.

"I saw your company just raised a Series B, congrats!" is not personalization. It's a trigger event pulled from Crunchbase that 40 other vendors also pulled. Your prospect received that exact opening line multiple times this month.

Real personalization requires understanding what someone actually cares about right now. Not what their company announced. What they're thinking about, struggling with, and actively discussing.

I'd suggest categorizing your personalization data into three tiers:

Tier 1 is firmographic and demographic data. Necessary for targeting but provides zero differentiation in messaging.

Tier 2 is trigger events and intent signals. Useful for timing, but the data is widely available. Don't lean on it for your opening line.

Tier 3 is content signals. What the person actually writes, shares, and comments on in their own words. This is where genuine personalization lives, and it's where most teams underinvest.

My advice: shift at least 50% of your personalization effort to Tier 3 signals. Yes, it's harder to systematize. But the response rates will justify the investment. Focus on the channels where your ICP talks openly about their challenges.

Outbound personalization in 2026 is mostly theater. The prospect knows it. You know it.

But you keep doing it because your VP of Sales saw a webinar about "personalization at scale" and now it's a quarterly OKR.

"I saw your company just raised a Series B, congrats!" Congrats on what? On receiving the same copy-paste opening line from 40 vendors who all subscribe to the same Crunchbase alerts?

Here's : most personalization isn't for the prospect. It's for the seller. It makes you feel like you did your homework. It lets your manager check a box. It gives your sequence tool a merge field to fill.

The prospect? They see right through it. Because they're getting 15 "personalized" emails a week that all reference the same three data points about their company. Different sender, same template energy.

Real personalization would mean actually understanding what the person cares about right now. Not what their company announced. What they personally are wrestling with.

But that requires reading their posts, engaging with their ideas, and crafting something that couldn't have been written by anyone else to anyone else.

That doesn't scale. Which is exactly why it works.

posts.title: Claude Code as GTM infrastructure
posts.hook: I run my entire content pipeline, CRM automation, and lead scoring from Claude Code and a cron job.
posts.body:
I run my entire content pipeline, CRM automation, and lead scoring from Claude Code and a cron job.

No Zapier. No Make. No n8n. Just Python scripts, a SQLite database, and an AI agent that knows my business context.

Here's what runs every night on my Mac Mini at midnight:

✅ Reddit scout pulls relevant posts from 6 subreddits
✅ AI scores each post for engagement opportunity
✅ Blog generator writes and publishes a daily post
✅ Content distributor queues drafts across platforms
✅ CRM enrichment updates Attio with fresh signal data

Total infrastructure cost: $0/month for compute. ~$15/month in API calls.

The dev tools conversation right now is all about Cursor and Replit and Devin for writing code. But the real unlock is using these same tools to build ops infrastructure that would cost you $500/month in SaaS fees.

Every integration platform charges you per step, per zap, per scenario. A Python script on a cron is free forever.

The tradeoff is real though. You need to be comfortable debugging at 2am when your script fails silently. There's no customer support to email. You are the platform team.
posts.cta: Are you building your GTM ops on SaaS platforms or custom scripts? What made you choose that path?

remixes.label: cost-breakdown
remixes.hook: My GTM stack costs $40/month. It replaced $2,400/month in SaaS subscriptions.
remixes.body:
My GTM stack costs $40/month. It replaced $2,400/month in SaaS subscriptions.

Here's the actual line-item breakdown:

$0/month: Reddit scout (local LLM scoring, cron-scheduled)
$0/month: Blog generator (runs nightly, writes to my site)
$0/month: Content distribution (Python scripts push to platforms via API)
$15/month: Enrichment API calls (Clay alternative, direct API)
$25/month: LLM API costs for drafting and scoring

What it replaced:
$300/month: Outreach platform
$200/month: Clay
$150/month: Zapier (yes, really)
$500/month: Content scheduling tool + ghost writer
$250/month: Lead scoring platform
$1,000/month: Intent data provider

The secret isn't that the custom stack is better engineered. It's that most GTM SaaS is selling you a UI wrapper around API calls you could make yourself. Once I realized that, I couldn't unsee it.

The hard part wasn't building. It was accepting that I'd been paying $2,400/month for convenience features I didn't actually need. The scheduling UI, the drag-and-drop workflow builder, the analytics dashboard. All nice-to-have. None essential.

A SQLite database, Python scripts, and a cron job running on a Mac Mini do the same work. Minus the monthly invoice.

I'm not saying this approach works for a 50-person sales team. But if you're a founder or solo GTM operator, you're almost certainly overpaying for tools that do less than you think.
remixes.cta: What's the most expensive tool in your GTM stack? Could you replace it with a script and an API key?

remixes.label: midnight-cron-story
remixes.hook: Every night at midnight, my Mac Mini wakes up and runs my entire GTM operation while I sleep.
remixes.body:
Every night at midnight, my Mac Mini wakes up and runs my entire GTM operation while I sleep.

It's not glamorous. No beautiful dashboard. No workflow canvas. Just a bash script that calls other scripts in sequence.

First, the Reddit scout scans six subreddits for conversations about problems I solve. A local LLM scores each post for relevance and engagement potential. High-scoring posts get queued for my review in the morning.

Then the blog generator kicks in. It reads my recent content, identifies a topic angle, writes a draft, and publishes it to my site. I review it over coffee.

After that, the enrichment pipeline runs. New leads from inbound forms and content engagement get enriched with firmographic data, scored against my ICP, and routed to the right queue in my CRM.

Finally, the SEO monitor checks my keyword rankings, logs changes, and flags any significant movement.

By the time I wake up, I have a queue of pre-scored Reddit conversations, a fresh blog post to review, enriched leads in my pipeline, and an SEO report.

Total infrastructure: one Mac Mini that sits on my desk. Total SaaS dependencies: zero. Total monthly cost: API calls.

The thing that surprised me most: this setup is more reliable than the SaaS tools it replaced. No vendor outages, no API rate limit surprises from platforms I don't control, no pricing changes. Just my code running on my hardware.
remixes.cta: What would you automate first if you had a dedicated machine running your GTM pipeline every night?

I run my entire content pipeline, CRM automation, and lead scoring from Claude Code and a cron job.

No Zapier. No Make. No n8n. Just Python scripts, a SQLite database, and an AI agent that knows my business context.

Here's what runs every night on my Mac Mini at midnight:

✅ Reddit scout pulls relevant posts from 6 subreddits
✅ AI scores each post for engagement opportunity
✅ Blog generator writes and publishes a daily post
✅ Content distributor queues drafts across platforms
✅ CRM enrichment updates Attio with fresh signal data

Total infrastructure cost: $0/month for compute. ~$15/month in API calls.

The dev tools conversation right now is all about Cursor and Replit and Devin for writing code. But the real unlock is using these same tools to build ops infrastructure that would cost you $500/month in SaaS fees.

Every integration platform charges you per step, per zap, per scenario. A Python script on a cron is free forever.

The tradeoff is real though. You need to be comfortable debugging at 2am when your script fails silently. There's no customer support to email. You are the platform team.

posts.title: RevOps unification is a myth
posts.hook: "Unified RevOps" is a slide deck. Not a real thing most teams have.
posts.body:
"Unified RevOps" is a slide deck. Not a real thing most teams have.

I've seen inside dozens of GTM stacks this year. Sales ops, marketing ops, and CS ops are still running on different tools with different data models and different definitions of what a "qualified" lead means.

The playbook says: shared KPIs, unified dashboards, single source of truth.

The reality: marketing counts MQLs their way, sales counts SQLs their way, CS counts expansion their way, and the board gets three different pipeline numbers depending on who presents.

The fix isn't better dashboards. It's fewer tools.

Every additional platform in your stack creates a new definition of truth. HubSpot thinks the lead came from the webinar. Salesforce thinks it came from the SDR. Marketo thinks it came from the nurture sequence. They're all right. They're all wrong.

I've started building stacks where one database is the canonical record. Everything else writes to it or reads from it. No bidirectional syncs. No "which system wins" debates.

It's less flexible. It's way more reliable.

The teams getting RevOps right aren't the ones with the best tools. They're the ones with the fewest tools.
posts.cta: How many sources of truth does your GTM stack actually have?

inspired_by.author: Briana McNally
inspired_by.summary: Details how RevOps teams can unify sales ops, marketing ops, and customer success using shared KPIs. Provides a playbook for implementing a revenue operations stack with tools like HubSpot and Snowflake. Highlights ROI from reducing tech stack silos.

remixes.label: three-truths-test
remixes.hook: Ask your sales, marketing, and CS teams to define a "qualified lead." You'll get three different answers.
remixes.body:
Ask your sales, marketing, and CS teams to define a "qualified lead." You'll get three different answers.

I've done this exercise with over a dozen GTM teams this year. The results are always the same.

Marketing says a qualified lead is someone who downloaded a whitepaper and matches firmographic criteria. Sales says it's someone who showed up to a demo with budget authority. CS says it's someone whose use case fits the product without requiring custom work.

All three definitions are reasonable. None of them agree. And each team is optimizing for their own definition while claiming they share KPIs.

This is what "unified RevOps" actually looks like inside most companies. Three teams with three dashboards pulling from three data sources, each telling a story that makes their numbers look good.

The fix isn't a new tool. It's a conversation. Get sales, marketing, and CS in a room. Ask one question: "What does a customer look like at the moment they close?" Work backwards from there.

You'll find that most of the qualifying criteria each team uses are proxies for something else. Marketing's whitepaper download is a proxy for problem awareness. Sales' budget authority is a proxy for deal velocity. CS's use case fit is a proxy for retention risk.

Map the proxies to the actual outcomes. Then build one scoring model that reflects all three. Not three models stitched together with Zapier.
remixes.cta: Have you ever run this test with your own GTM teams? What did you find?

remixes.label: data-model-warfare
remixes.hook: Your GTM stack doesn't have a tool problem. It has a data model problem.
remixes.body:
Your GTM stack doesn't have a tool problem. It has a data model problem.

I've been inside dozens of GTM stacks this year. The tools are usually fine. Salesforce works. HubSpot works. Outreach works. The problem is that each tool defines the same concepts differently.

In your CRM, a "contact" is a person at a company. In your marketing automation, a "contact" is an email address with engagement history. In your support tool, a "contact" is a ticket creator. Same word, three different objects with three different schemas.

This is why your "unified dashboard" is actually three dashboards bolted together with integrations that silently break when someone renames a field.

I've seen teams spend six months building a "single source of truth" by syncing data between tools. They end up with a data warehouse that's technically unified but practically useless because the same lead shows up three times with different lifecycle stages depending on which system wrote the record last.

The uncomfortable solution: pick one tool as the authority for each concept. Your CRM is the authority for account and contact data. Your marketing platform is the authority for engagement data. Your support tool is the authority for ticket data. Everything else subscribes.

No syncing. No bidirectional updates. One direction, one authority. It's not elegant. But it's the only architecture I've seen that actually works past month three.
remixes.cta: How many times does the same lead appear in your stack with conflicting data? Be honest.

tones.advisor:
"Unified RevOps" is a slide deck. Not a real thing most teams have.

Here's what I've observed across dozens of GTM stacks this year, and my recommendations for teams trying to actually unify.

Sales ops, marketing ops, and CS ops are still running on different tools with different data models and different definitions of what a "qualified" lead means. The playbook says shared KPIs, unified dashboards, single source of truth. The reality is fragmented.

Marketing counts MQLs their way. Sales qualifies with different criteria. CS measures health with their own scoring. Each team reports metrics that make their function look successful, and nobody reconciles the differences.

My recommendation for teams serious about fixing this:

First, run a definitions audit. Get each team to write down their definition of key terms: qualified lead, opportunity, active account, churn risk. Compare them side by side. The gaps will be immediately obvious.

Second, pick one system as the authority for each data type. Your CRM owns account data. Marketing automation owns engagement data. No bidirectional syncing. One source, many consumers.

Third, build one shared dashboard that reports on revenue outcomes, not activity metrics. MQLs and SALs are process metrics that each team defines differently. Revenue is revenue. Start there and work backwards.
tones.provocateur:
"Unified RevOps" is a slide deck. Not a real thing most teams have.

And the RevOps vendors selling you the "unified" dream know this. They just need you to believe it's one more integration away.

I've seen inside dozens of GTM stacks this year. Every single one claims to have a single source of truth. Every single one actually has three or four, each maintained by a different team, each telling a different story about the same data.

Marketing counts MQLs their way. Sales counts them differently. And when someone finally reconciles the numbers, they find that 30% of "qualified" leads in the marketing dashboard don't even exist in the CRM.

But nobody talks about it. Because admitting your data is fragmented means admitting that the $200k you spent on RevOps tooling didn't actually solve the problem. It just moved the mess from spreadsheets into platforms that are harder to audit.

The dirty secret of RevOps: the problem was never technology. It was that sales, marketing, and CS have fundamentally different incentives, and no amount of dashboard unification changes that.

You don't need another integration. You need your three ops leaders in a room agreeing on what words mean. Until that happens, every tool you buy is just a more expensive way to disagree.

posts.title: Building in public is the new outbound
posts.hook: Building in public replaced outbound as my top pipeline source. I didn't plan it that way.
posts.body:
Building in public replaced outbound as my top pipeline source. I didn't plan it that way.

For 6 months I've been posting build logs, sharing architecture decisions, open-sourcing scripts, and documenting what works and what breaks in my GTM automation stack.

No pitch. No "DM me for details." Just... here's what I built today, here's why, here's the code.

The result: inbound from people who already understand what I do, already trust the work, and already have a specific problem they want solved. The sales cycle on these deals is basically zero.

Compare that to cold outbound where you're spending the first three calls just establishing that you know what you're talking about.

KP documented 12 weeks of building his tool in public and launched to an audience that was already invested in the outcome. That's not marketing. That's compound interest on trust.

The catch: it only works if you're actually building something real. You can't fake build logs. People spot performative "transparency" immediately.

If your work is genuinely interesting and you show it honestly, the audience self-selects. The people who engage are exactly the people who might buy.
posts.cta: Have you ever hired someone or bought something specifically because you watched them build it?

inspired_by.author: KP (Karthik Pattabiraman)
inspired_by.summary: KP documented week 12 of building his AI-powered newsletter tool in public, announcing its launch after 3 months of solo development. He broke down revenue from day one, tech decisions, and advice for other builders starting out. Highlights the power of consistent public updates.

remixes.label: trust-arbitrage
remixes.hook: The fastest way to build trust with a buyer is to let them watch you work before you ever get on a call.
remixes.body:
The fastest way to build trust with a buyer is to let them watch you work before you ever get on a call.

I stopped sending cold emails 6 months ago. Instead, I started posting the actual work. Build logs. Architecture decisions. Scripts that solve real problems. Screenshots of dashboards I built for my own GTM stack.

No gating. No lead magnets. No "comment AUTOMATION and I'll DM you the template."

Just work, in the open, every day.

What happened: prospects started showing up to discovery calls having already read 15 of my posts. They'd reference specific scripts. They'd say "I saw how you wired Clay to Attio and we need exactly that."

The sales cycle compressed from weeks to days. Not because I got better at selling. Because the selling already happened before the call.

Traditional outbound asks strangers to trust you based on a cold email. Building in public lets people watch you solve problems like theirs, in real time, for months. By the time they reach out, you're not a vendor. You're the person who already proved they know what they're doing.

The trust gap that outbound tries to bridge with sequences and follow-ups? Public work closes it before the first conversation.
remixes.cta: Think about your last big purchase decision. Did you buy from someone you'd been following, or a cold pitch?

remixes.label: content-as-qualifying
remixes.hook: My content doesn't generate leads. It generates qualified buyers who already understand my pricing.
remixes.body:
My content doesn't generate leads. It generates qualified buyers who already understand my pricing.

There's a massive difference. Leads fill your pipeline with noise. Qualified buyers fill it with people ready to move.

Here's how it works: when you post detailed build logs showing exactly how you solve problems, two things happen simultaneously. People who can't afford you self-select out because they see the complexity. People who need what you do self-select in because they recognize the problem.

I post about wiring GTM automation stacks. The CRM integrations, the scoring logic, the data enrichment pipelines. All of it, publicly.

The person running a 5-person startup looks at that and thinks "I'll just do it myself with the scripts he shared." Great. They were never a buyer.

The VP of RevOps at a 200-person company looks at it and thinks "we need this built for us, and this person clearly knows how." They book a call.

I didn't build a funnel. I built a filter. Every post qualifies or disqualifies the reader before they ever contact me. My close rate went up not because I got better at closing, but because I stopped getting on calls with the wrong people.
remixes.cta: Is your content attracting the right buyers, or just the most buyers?

posts.title: The agent stack nobody talks about
posts.hook: The AI agent stack conversation is missing the boring layer that actually matters.
posts.body:
The AI agent stack conversation is missing the boring layer that actually matters.

Everyone's debating orchestration frameworks. LangChain vs CrewAI vs custom. Which LLM. Which vector store. Which memory system.

Nobody's talking about the cron job.

My most reliable AI agents aren't running on fancy orchestration platforms. They're Python scripts triggered by launchd on a Mac Mini. They run at midnight. They write results to SQLite. They send me a summary in the morning.

No Kubernetes. No Lambda. No container orchestration. Just a computer that's always on, running scripts on a schedule.

The orchestration layer matters when you're building a product. When you're building internal ops automation, the execution layer is a solved problem. It's called cron.

Here's what actually breaks in production AI agents:

1. API rate limits at scale nobody tested for
2. LLM output format changing between model versions
3. Upstream data sources going down with no fallback
4. Token costs 10x what you estimated because context windows bloat

None of these are orchestration problems. They're all engineering discipline problems. The same problems we solved in backend systems 20 years ago.

Stop shopping for frameworks. Start shipping scripts.
posts.cta: What's your AI agent actually running on? Cloud infra or something simpler?

inspired_by.author: Amanda Scalzi
inspired_by.summary: Scalzi breaks down the AI agent stack, from orchestration layers to execution engines. Discusses automation in enterprise settings and essential developer tools for building custom agents. Highlights infrastructure bottlenecks like compute costs.

remixes.label: infra-over-intelligence
remixes.hook: My dumbest AI agent makes me the most money. It's a Python script that runs at 2 AM.
remixes.body:
My dumbest AI agent makes me the most money. It's a Python script that runs at 2 AM.

No LangChain. No vector store. No memory system. No orchestration framework. It reads a JSON file, calls an API, writes results to a database, and sends a Slack message if something looks off.

It runs on a Mac Mini via launchd. It's been running for months without a single failure.

Meanwhile, I've watched teams spend 6 weeks building "intelligent" agent systems on Kubernetes clusters with sophisticated retry logic and observability dashboards. Their agents go down every few days because some dependency update broke the chain.

The AI agent conversation has a blind spot. Everyone's optimizing for intelligence when they should be optimizing for reliability. The fanciest reasoning chain in the world is worthless if the thing crashes at 3 AM and nobody notices until Monday.

My most valuable agents share three traits: they run on a schedule, they fail loudly, and they recover without human intervention. None of those traits require a framework. They require good old-fashioned cron discipline.

The boring infrastructure layer, scheduling, error handling, state management, logging, is where agent value actually compounds. Not in the model choice. Not in the prompt engineering. In whether the thing runs when it's supposed to.
remixes.cta: What's your most reliable automation? Bet it's also your simplest.

remixes.label: framework-trap
remixes.hook: Every AI agent framework adds complexity. The question nobody asks: does this agent need a framework at all?
remixes.body:
Every AI agent framework adds complexity. The question nobody asks: does this agent need a framework at all?

I run a fleet of AI agents for GTM automation. Content generation, data enrichment, lead scoring, competitive monitoring. They produce real business output daily.

Not a single one uses an orchestration framework.

They're Python scripts. Some call Claude's API directly. Some call multiple APIs in sequence. They run on launchd schedules on a Mac Mini. Total infrastructure cost: the electricity bill.

Here's what I've learned: most "agent" use cases are actually just automated workflows with an LLM call in the middle. You don't need agent memory when you can read from a database. You don't need tool-use frameworks when you can just call the API. You don't need orchestration when cron handles sequencing.

Frameworks solve real problems, but only at scale most teams haven't reached. If you have 3-5 agents doing defined tasks on schedules, a framework adds overhead without adding value.

The litmus test: can you describe what your agent does in one sentence? If yes, you probably don't need a framework. If the answer involves "dynamically deciding" or "reasoning about which tools to use," maybe you do.

Start with scripts. Add complexity only when scripts break down. Most teams never reach that point.
remixes.cta: Honest question: is your agent framework solving a problem you actually have, or one you might have someday?

tones.builder:
The AI agent stack conversation is missing the boring layer that actually matters.

Everyone's debating orchestration frameworks. LangChain vs CrewAI vs custom. Which LLM. Which vector store. Which memory system.

Nobody's talking about the cron job.

My most reliable AI agents aren't running on fancy orchestration platforms. They're Python scripts triggered by launchd on a Mac Mini. They run at midnight. They write results to SQLite. They send me a summary in the morning.

No Kubernetes. No Lambda. No container orchestration. Just a computer that's always on, running scripts on a schedule.

The orchestration layer matters when you're building a product. When you're building internal ops automation, the execution layer is a solved problem. It's called cron.

Here's what actually breaks in production AI agents:

1. API rate limits at scale nobody tested for
2. LLM output format changing between model versions
3. Upstream data sources going down with no fallback
4. Token costs 10x what you estimated because context windows bloat

None of these are orchestration problems. They're all engineering discipline problems. The same problems we solved in backend systems 20 years ago.

Stop shopping for frameworks. Start shipping scripts.

posts.title: 60% automation is the wrong goal
posts.hook: "We automated 60% of manual sales tasks with AI." Cool. Which 60%?
posts.body:
"We automated 60% of manual sales tasks with AI." Cool. Which 60%?

Because if you automated data entry, CRM updates, and activity logging... you didn't save your reps time. You saved them from work they were already skipping.

The 60% number is a vanity metric. What matters is which tasks you automated and whether reps are now spending that time on activities that close deals.

I've seen teams automate their entire outbound sequence and then wonder why pipeline didn't grow. The sequences were already running. Automating them just made bad emails go out faster.

Here's what actually moves numbers when you automate sales ops:

✅ Research that reps won't do manually (enrichment, technographics, org charts)
✅ Routing that eliminates "who owns this account" debates
✅ Scoring that surfaces the 20 accounts worth calling today out of 2,000
✅ Follow-up triggers based on real buying signals, not arbitrary cadence timers

These aren't the easy automations. They require good data, clear ICP definitions, and someone who understands both the sales process and the technical implementation.

That's why most "AI for sales" tools underdeliver. They automate the easy stuff and leave the hard stuff untouched.
posts.cta: What's one manual task your sales team still does that you haven't been able to automate well?

inspired_by.author: Kyle Coleman
inspired_by.summary: Covers automating sales ops workflows with AI to cut manual tasks by 60%. Discusses integrating outbound sequences with CRM for real-time forecasting. Includes tips on compliance and scaling during hypergrowth.

remixes.label: automation-audit
remixes.hook: I audited a team's "AI-powered sales stack" last month. They'd automated 14 tasks. Reps were doing 2 of them before the automation existed.
remixes.body:
I audited a team's "AI-powered sales stack" last month. They'd automated 14 tasks. Reps were doing 2 of them before the automation existed.

The other 12? CRM field updates nobody checked. Activity logs nobody read. Data enrichment that populated fields reps never opened.

They'd spent 4 months and a mid-six-figure budget automating work that wasn't happening in the first place. Then they presented the project as "85% task automation" to the board.

This is the automation theater problem. Teams measure success by counting what they automated, not by measuring what changed in rep behavior or pipeline output.

Here's what actually matters: after automation, are reps spending more time on activities that directly correlate with closed revenue? Not "tasks completed" or "hours saved." Actual pipeline movement.

The honest audit looks like this. List every manual task. Mark which ones reps actually do consistently. Automate only those. Then measure whether reps redirected that time to selling or just found new ways to avoid prospecting.

The uncomfortable finding from every automation audit I've done: the biggest time sink for most reps isn't manual tasks. It's context switching between 8 different tools. Automating tasks inside those tools doesn't fix that. Consolidating the tools does.

Automate the right 20% and you'll outperform the team that automated 80%.
remixes.cta: When was the last time you checked whether your reps were actually doing the tasks you automated?

remixes.label: outcome-first-automation
remixes.hook: Stop measuring automation by tasks eliminated. Start measuring it by revenue activities gained.
remixes.body:
Stop measuring automation by tasks eliminated. Start measuring it by revenue activities gained.

The standard automation pitch: "We'll eliminate X hours of manual work per rep per week." Sounds great. But hours eliminated and hours reinvested are very different things.

I worked with a team that automated their entire lead routing and CRM update workflow. Saved reps an estimated 6 hours per week. Net result after 3 months: zero change in meetings booked, pipeline created, or close rates.

Where did the 6 hours go? Email. Slack. Internal meetings. The time got absorbed into organizational entropy, not redirected to selling.

The teams that actually get ROI from automation do something different. They don't just automate away tasks. They explicitly redesign the rep workflow to fill the gap with specific revenue activities.

"We automated CRM updates" becomes "We automated CRM updates AND added 4 prospecting blocks per week to every rep's calendar with pre-loaded target lists."

The automation itself is half the project. The other half is workflow redesign to capture the freed time. Skip the second half and you get a great case study with no revenue impact.

Measure automation success by one metric: did reps increase the number of revenue-generating activities per week? Everything else is a vanity dashboard.
remixes.cta: Has your team actually measured what reps do with the time automation freed up?

"We automated 60% of manual sales tasks with AI." That's a number I see in a lot of pitch decks and board presentations. Here's the question I always ask: which 60%?

Because if you automated data entry, CRM updates, and activity logging, you need to examine whether reps were consistently doing those tasks in the first place. In my experience, most weren't. You didn't save them time. You formalized work they'd already deprioritized.

The 60% number is a vanity metric. What I recommend focusing on instead: which specific tasks were automated, and is there measurable evidence that reps are now spending that recaptured time on activities that move pipeline?

Here's the framework I use with teams. First, audit which manual tasks reps actually perform consistently, not which ones exist in your process docs. Second, automate only those. Third, explicitly design what reps should do with the freed time, don't leave it to chance. Fourth, measure revenue activity volume before and after.

The teams that see real automation ROI aren't the ones with the highest percentage of tasks automated. They're the ones who paired automation with deliberate workflow redesign to channel freed capacity into selling activities.

If you can't point to an increase in meetings booked, proposals sent, or deals advanced, your automation project is a technology success and a revenue miss.

Here's what actually happened: someone on the RevOps team needed a win. They counted every task in the CRM workflow, automated the easy ones that require zero judgment, and slapped a percentage on a slide deck. The board clapped. Pipeline didn't move.

But nobody gets promoted for saying "we automated 8% of sales tasks, but it was the right 8%, and pipeline velocity increased 23%."

The automation-industrial complex needs big numbers. So we get big numbers. 60% automated. 40% time saved. Zero revenue impact, but hey, the dashboard looks incredible.

Stop automating for coverage. Start automating for outcomes.

posts.title: Going solo after big tech
posts.hook: 3 months ago I walked away from a comfortable situation to build GTM automation as an independent.
posts.body:
3 months ago I walked away from a comfortable situation to build GTM automation as an independent.

Not because I had a grand vision. Because the gap between what companies need and what agencies deliver is enormous, and I kept seeing it from the inside.

Companies buy a "GTM platform" and get a dashboard. They need someone who can wire Clay to Attio to their outbound sequences with custom scoring logic that actually reflects how their buyers behave.

That's not a product. That's a practitioner.

The Daniel Vassallo "small bets" model resonates but I'd frame it differently. It's not about making small bets. It's about having small overhead so you can say no to bad-fit work and yes to interesting problems.

My entire infrastructure runs on a Mac Mini. My tools are open source or cheap. My content pipeline is automated. My CRM is Attio. Monthly burn is under $200.

That means I don't need to take every client. I can pick the ones where I genuinely know how to help and where the work is interesting enough to write about.

3 months in, the pipeline is healthy and the work is better than anything I did with a bigger team around me.
posts.cta: For those who went independent... what's the one thing you wish someone told you before you made the jump?

inspired_by.author: Daniel Vassallo
inspired_by.summary: Daniel posted about launching his latest small bet product after pivoting from big tech, built solo in under a month. He shared honest revenue numbers, marketing tactics that worked, and encouragement for others considering tech career shifts to independent building.

remixes.label: gap-spotter
remixes.hook: I didn't leave to "follow my passion." I left because I kept seeing the same gap from the inside and nobody was filling it.
remixes.body:
I didn't leave to "follow my passion." I left because I kept seeing the same gap from the inside and nobody was filling it.

Every company I worked with had the same problem. They'd buy GTM tools, sometimes 5 or 6 of them, then realize nobody on their team could actually wire them together. So they'd hire an agency. The agency would set up basic integrations and charge monthly retainers for work that took 3 hours.

The gap isn't tools. It's the person who understands both the business logic and the technical implementation. Someone who can sit with a RevOps leader, understand their scoring model, then go build it across Clay, Attio, and their outbound sequences. Same day. Not a 6-week SOW.

Three months in, here's what I've learned: the market for this is real but invisible. Companies don't post job listings for "GTM automation engineer." They don't know that role exists. They post for "RevOps manager" and hope that person can also code. Or they hire a dev and hope they understand pipeline mechanics.

Going independent wasn't a lifestyle choice. It was a bet that this gap is big enough to build a practice around. The early evidence says yes. But the hardest part isn't the work. It's explaining what you do to people who've never seen it done.
remixes.cta: Have you ever spotted a gap in your industry that nobody seemed to be filling? What did you do about it?

remixes.label: honest-inventory
remixes.hook: Here's what nobody preparing to go solo actually wants to hear: the first 90 days are about unlearning, not building.
remixes.body:
Here's what nobody preparing to go solo actually wants to hear: the first 90 days are about unlearning, not building.

I went independent 3 months ago to build GTM automation. I had the skills, the network, the portfolio. I figured I'd hit the ground running.

Instead, I spent the first month unlearning habits that made me effective inside a company but are useless on your own.

At a company, you optimize for internal consensus. Solo, consensus is a trap. You either move or you don't. Nobody's validating your roadmap.

At a company, you have built-in distribution. Your Slack messages reach 200 people. Solo, you're shouting into the void until you build an audience from zero.

At a company, scope is defined for you. Solo, every opportunity looks like the right one. Saying no is harder than any technical challenge.

Month two was rebuilding those muscles. Writing publicly every day to build distribution. Defining a tight service offering instead of saying yes to everything. Building systems to replace the structure a company provides.

Month three is where it started clicking. Pipeline from content. Referrals from the network. A rhythm that actually works.

The gap between comfortable employment and functional independence isn't about skills or courage. It's about the operating system in your head that needs a complete reinstall.

3 months ago I walked away from a comfortable situation to build GTM automation as an independent. I want to share what I've learned, especially for those considering a similar move.

The catalyst wasn't a grand vision. It was a persistent gap I observed from the inside: the distance between what companies need from their GTM stack and what agencies or platforms actually deliver.

Companies buy a "GTM platform" and get a dashboard. They need someone who can wire Clay to Attio to their outbound sequences with custom scoring logic. They need an engineer who understands business process, not just API documentation.

Here's what I'd recommend if you're considering going independent in a technical GTM role. First, validate that your skill set solves a problem people will pay for, not just a problem they complain about. Second, start building your public presence 3 to 6 months before you leave. The pipeline you need on day one takes months to develop. Third, define your offering tightly. "I do GTM automation" is too broad. "I wire your enrichment and scoring pipeline across your existing tools" is a service people can buy.

The market for hands-on GTM engineering is real and growing. But the transition from employee to independent requires more operational rewiring than most people expect. Build the infrastructure before you need it.

3 months ago I walked away from a comfortable situation to build GTM automation as an independent. And the thing that pushed me out the door? Watching agencies charge $15k/month for work I could do in an afternoon.

Let's be honest about what the GTM services industry actually looks like from the inside. Companies buy platforms that promise automation and get dashboards full of metrics nobody acts on. Then they hire agencies to "optimize" those platforms, and the agencies build basic Zapier workflows and call it a custom solution.

The gap between what companies need and what they're getting is not a crack. It's a canyon. They need someone who can look at their revenue process, understand the business logic, and build the actual technical infrastructure. Same person. Same brain. Not a "strategy consultant" who hands off a PDF to a junior dev in another timezone.

Going independent wasn't brave. Staying would have been. Staying meant watching this gap widen while pretending the industry's approach was working.

Three months in, every conversation confirms the thesis. Companies are desperate for people who can actually build, not just advise. The agencies know it too. That's why they're all frantically hiring engineers and calling themselves "GTM engineering firms" now.

The market doesn't need more GTM strategists. It needs builders who understand business.

posts.title: Content strategy that builds pipeline
posts.hook: Most B2B content strategies optimize for impressions. The ones that work optimize for saved posts.
posts.body:
Most B2B content strategies optimize for impressions. The ones that work optimize for saved posts.

Here's the difference:

Impression content: hot takes, memes, engagement bait. Gets likes. Gets reach. Gets zero pipeline.

Saved content: tactical breakdowns, real workflows, copy-paste prompts, architecture diagrams. Gets bookmarked. Gets shared in Slack channels. Gets forwarded to the person who actually buys.

I track which of my posts generate inbound conversations. The pattern is clear:

🔧 Posts that show exactly how I built something, with enough detail to replicate it, drive 5x more inbound than posts about why you should build it.

The 3x pipeline growth frameworks floating around right now mostly boil down to this: stop writing content that makes people think "that's interesting" and start writing content that makes people think "I need to try this."

One post showing my exact Clay enrichment workflow with column names and formulas generated more qualified conversations than a month of thought leadership.

The counterintuitive part: giving away your methodology doesn't cost you clients. It attracts the ones who see the work involved and decide they'd rather hire you than DIY it.

Substance is the strategy. Everything else is distribution.
posts.cta: What's the last piece of content you bookmarked because you actually wanted to use it?

inspired_by.author: Anika Agrawal
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down a content strategy framework for B2B SaaS that drives 3x pipeline growth. Focuses on audience-first content clusters integrated with GTM motions. Shares case study of generating $2M in opportunities from LinkedIn and newsletters.

remixes.label: save-rate-metric
remixes.hook: I track one content metric that most marketers ignore: save rate. It predicts pipeline better than impressions, likes, or comments combined.
remixes.body:
I track one content metric that most marketers ignore: save rate. It predicts pipeline better than impressions, likes, or comments combined.

Here's why. A like is a reflex. A comment is social. A share is performative. But a save? A save means someone thought "I need this later." That's intent. That's someone filing your content into their workflow.

I analyzed 6 months of my LinkedIn posts against inbound conversations. The correlation between impressions and pipeline was nearly zero. The correlation between save rate and qualified inbound was striking.

Posts with high saves and low impressions outperformed viral posts with zero saves every single time.

The types of content that get saved: step-by-step technical breakdowns. Copy-paste prompt templates. Architecture diagrams with labels. Workflow screenshots with annotations. Comparison tables with honest assessments.

The common thread: utility. Saved content is content someone plans to reference while doing actual work. It enters their Slack channels, their Notion boards, their team wikis. It travels through dark social to the actual decision maker.

When I redesigned my content strategy around save rate, everything changed. I stopped optimizing for hooks and started optimizing for reference value. Fewer posts, but each one designed to be bookmarked.

The result: half the impressions, triple the inbound quality.
remixes.cta: Go check your last 10 posts. Which ones got the most saves? I bet they're not the same ones that got the most likes.

remixes.label: dark-social-pipeline
remixes.hook: The content that builds your pipeline doesn't perform well on the feed. It performs well in DMs, Slack channels, and email forwards.
remixes.body:
The content that builds your pipeline doesn't perform well on the feed. It performs well in DMs, Slack channels, and email forwards.

I spent months chasing LinkedIn impressions. Hot takes, engagement bait, controversy. The numbers were great. The pipeline was empty.

Then I started posting tactical content. Real workflows. Actual architecture diagrams. Scripts people could copy and use. The impressions dropped by 60%. The inbound tripled.

The difference: dark social. Content that gets forwarded privately to the person who actually needs it.

A VP of RevOps doesn't publicly engage with your post about GTM automation. They screenshot it and send it to their team lead in Slack with "can we do this?" That's how deals start. Not from a like or a comment.

The content that travels through dark social has specific traits. It's reference material, not entertainment. It solves a real problem. It has enough detail that someone can act on it or forward it to someone who can. It doesn't need the feed's context to make sense.

Most B2B content strategies are optimized for the algorithm. The ones that generate pipeline are optimized for the screenshot. For the Slack forward. For the "sent this to my CTO" DM.

The question isn't "will this get engagement?" It's "will someone send this to their boss?"

That reframe changed everything about how I create content.
remixes.cta: Think about the last piece of content you forwarded to a colleague. What made it worth sharing privately?

Most B2B content strategies optimize for impressions. The ones that actually drive pipeline optimize for something different: saved posts and private forwards.

Here's the framework I'd recommend for thinking about content that converts.

Impression content, which includes hot takes, memes, and engagement bait, gets likes and reach. It performs well on the surface. But it generates virtually zero pipeline because it doesn't travel to decision makers through the channels that matter.

Saved content, which includes tactical breakdowns, real workflows, copy-paste templates, and architecture diagrams, gets bookmarked. It gets shared in Slack channels. It gets forwarded to the person who controls budget.

The strategic principle: content that someone saves is content they plan to reference while doing actual work. That's the strongest buying signal available on social platforms.

My recommendation: audit your last 20 posts. Separate them by save rate, not impressions. You'll likely find that your highest-performing posts by engagement are completely different from your highest-performing posts by inbound quality.

Then redesign your content calendar around one question: "Would someone bookmark this to reference later?" If the answer is no, it might get engagement, but it won't build pipeline. Prioritize utility over virality, and your content becomes a consistent source of qualified inbound.

Stop writing content for the algorithm. Start writing content for the screenshot. For the Slack forward. For the "sent this to my CTO" DM. That's where deals start. Not in your comments section.
